meta.title: What are truffles, and why are they so expensive?
meta.speaker: Carolyn Beans
meta.youtubeTitle: What are truffles, and why are they so expensive? - Carolyn Beans

sentences.text: In autumn 2007, a Tuscan father and son unearthed one of the largest white truffles ever found.
sentences.translation: 在2007年秋天一对托斯卡纳的父子发掘了 有史以来最大的一块白松露。

sentences.text: It later sold at auction for a record-breaking $330,000— more than six times the price of its weight in gold at the time.
sentences.translation: 后来，它在拍卖会上 以创纪录的价格售出33万美元——是当时其等重量黄金价格的六倍多。

sentences.text: Truffles are one of the world’s most expensive foods— in part because global demand often outstrips supply.
sentences.translation: 松露是世界上最昂贵的食品之一 ——部分原因是全球需求往往超过其供应。

sentences.text: But why are truffles so rare?
sentences.translation: 但为什么松露是如此稀有？

sentences.text: And why don't we just farm more of them?
sentences.translation: 那为什么我们不多种一些呢？

sentences.text: The answers lie in the truffle’s unique and somewhat mysterious biology.
sentences.translation: 答案在于松露独特且有些神秘的生物学特性。

sentences.text: Truffles refer to nearly 200 species of fungi, of which around 30 are used in commercial trade.
sentences.translation: 松露指的是近200种物种真菌，其中约30种用于商业贸易。

sentences.text: The most sought-after truffles are native to Europe, confined to regions with lime-rich dry soil and light summer rains.
sentences.translation: 最受追捧的松露原产于欧洲，产地仅限于富含石灰的干燥土壤 和有轻微夏季降雨的地区。

sentences.text: Each species grows under the canopy of specific trees, where they form tight symbiotic relationships.
sentences.translation: 每个物种都生长在特定树木的树冠下，在那里它们形成紧密的共生关系。

sentences.text: A truffle’s mycelium, the vegetative root-like structure of the fungus, wraps itself around the tree roots and swaps water and nutrients for sugars.
sentences.translation: 松露的菌丝体，是真菌的营养根状结构，它将自己包裹在树根上， 并将水和营养物质换成糖。

sentences.text: While truffles themselves aren’t mushrooms, the parts of truffles we covet are, like mushrooms, the fruiting body of the fungus.
sentences.translation: 虽然松露本身不是蘑菇，但我们垂涎的松露部分， 像蘑菇一样，是真菌的子实体。

sentences.text: But while mushrooms grow above ground, allowing their spores to be dispersed by both wind and foraging animals, truffles grow entirely below the soil.
sentences.translation: 但是，当蘑菇生长在地上时，使它们的孢子可以通过风和觅食动物散播，而松露则完全生长在土壤之下。

sentences.text: So, they release a powerful aroma to lure hungry forest animals to come to do their spreading.
sentences.translation: 因此，它们释放出一种强大的芳香剂， 引诱饥饿的森林动物前来传播（孢子）。

sentences.text: If these animals happen to drop these spores near the roots of a particular type of tree, only then might a truffle develop, several years later.
sentences.translation: 如果这些动物碰巧将这些孢子落在特定类型树的根部附近，只有在那之后，松露才可能生长出来， 通常是在几年后。

sentences.text: This pungent aroma is also what’s made truffles alluring to humans.
sentences.translation: 这种强烈的香气也是松露吸引人类的原因。

sentences.text: In fact, much of what we perceive as truffle flavor comes from its unique scent.
sentences.translation: 实际上，我们所认为的松露味道在很大程度上来自其独特的香味。

sentences.text: A single species typically emits 30 to 60 different volatile compounds, which are produced by both the truffle and the bacteria and yeast that colonize them.
sentences.translation: 一个物种通常会释放 30 到 60种 不同的挥发性化合物，这些化合物是由松露以及定殖在 其上的细菌和酵母菌共同产生的。

sentences.text: But despite their scent, finding these subterranean gems within a dense forest is far from easy.
sentences.translation: 但是，尽管它们的气味在茂密的森林中 找到这些地下宝石绝非易事。

sentences.text: As early as the 1400s, Europeans used pigs as truffle-finding companions.
sentences.translation: 早在 1400 年代，欧洲人就使用猪 作为寻找松露的伙伴。

sentences.text: Truffles emit dimethyl sulfide, a compound that reeks of cabbage, which helps draw the pigs in.
sentences.translation: 松露会释放二甲基硫化物， 这种化合物会散发像卷心菜的味道，有助于吸引猪。

sentences.text: But when a pig finds a truffle, they’re apt to take a bite out of the profit.
sentences.translation: 但是，当猪找到松露时，它们很容易从利润中分一杯羹。

sentences.text: So many truffle hunters began replacing pigs with dogs who, while not natural-born truffle hunters, are more easily trained.
sentences.translation: 许多松露猎人开始用狗代替猪，这些狗虽然不是天生的松露猎手， 但它们更容易训练。

sentences.text: Meanwhile, others learn to track their prize by following species of flies that are known to lay their eggs near truffles.
sentences.translation: 与此同时，其他人通过跟踪一些已知会 在松露附近产卵的苍蝇种类，来寻找他们的奖励。

sentences.text: Nevertheless, today, the global market still demands more truffles than any pig, dog, or fly can sniff out.
sentences.translation: 然而，今天，全球市场对松露的需求仍然超过了任何猪、狗或苍蝇能够嗅出的数量。

sentences.text: And truffles continue to grow even rarer— and more expensive— as deforestation and climate change shrink their suitable terrain.
sentences.translation: 松露持续变得愈加稀有——也愈加昂贵——随着森林砍伐和气候变化缩小了 它们适宜生长的区域

sentences.text: So why don't we just farm them?
sentences.translation: 那么，为什么我们不直接种植它们呢？

sentences.text: We do, to some extent.
sentences.translation: 在某种程度上，我们确实如此。

sentences.text: In 1808, a French farmer became the first to cultivate truffles when he planted acorns under truffle-bearing oaks, and then transplanted the seedlings with— unknowingly at the time— some fungi companions.
sentences.translation: 1808 年，一位法国农民 成为第一个种植松露的人，他在含有松露的橡树下种下橡子，然后将幼苗移植到——当时并不知道——一些真菌伴侣身上。

sentences.text: By the early 1970s, scientists cracked how to artificially inoculate tree seedlings, which allowed growers to sow certain species of truffles in orchards far outside their native range.
sentences.translation: 到 1970 年代初，科学家们破解了如何人工嫁接树苗，这使得种植者能够在远离原产地的果园里种植某些种类的松露。

sentences.text: However, truffles are still not considered a domesticated crop.
sentences.translation: 但是，松露仍不被视为一种被驯化的作物。

sentences.text: There are just too many unknowns, as researchers are still working out the precise temperature, precipitation, elevation, soil pH, and other environmental factors that determine a truffle orchard’s success.
sentences.translation: 因为还有太多未知的因素，因为研究人员仍在努力研究 精确的温度、降水量、海拔、土壤酸碱值及其他环境因素这些都会决定松露果园成功的因素。

sentences.text: Part of the problem is that truffles need to mate to form that valuable fruiting body, yet truffles sex is still largely a mystery.
sentences.translation: 问题的一部分在于 松露需要交配才能形成宝贵的子实体，但松露的繁殖在很大程度上仍然是个谜。

sentences.text: Researchers know that these fungi are hermaphrodites that cannot reproduce alone.
sentences.translation: 研究人员知道，这些真菌是无法单独繁殖的雌雄同体。

sentences.text: So, when a truffle reproduces, one fungus takes on the maternal role and the other the paternal role, each contributing half of the genes that will form the spores.
sentences.translation: 因此，当松露繁殖时，一种真菌承担了母性的角色， 而另一种则承担了父性的角色，每种真菌都贡献了形成孢子的基因的一半。

sentences.text: However, when researchers search the area near a truffle fruiting body, they only ever find maternal mycelium.
sentences.translation: 但是，当研究人员搜索松露子实体 附近的区域时，他们只能找到母体菌丝体。

sentences.text: Where the paternal partner lives— or hides— remains unknown.
sentences.translation: 雄性伙伴的栖息地—— 或者说藏身之处——仍然未知。

sentences.text: So each time a hopeful truffle enthusiast begins a new farm, there’s no guarantee truffles will grow, and they'll have to wait four years or more to find out.
sentences.translation: 因此，每当一个充满希望的 松露爱好者开始经营一个新的农场时，松露是否能够生长并没有保证，而且他们必须等四年或更长时间才能得到结果。

sentences.text: But many take the gamble, as growers know that if they’re patient and lucky, they might just dig up gold.
sentences.translation: 但是许多人选择了这场赌博，因为种植者知道，如果他们有耐心和幸运的话，他们可能会挖出黄金。

cues.text: In autumn 2007,
a Tuscan father and son unearthed one of the largest white truffles ever found.
It later sold at auction for a record-breaking $330,000—
more than six times the price of its weight in gold at the time.
Truffles are one of the world’s most expensive foods—
in part because global demand often outstrips supply.
But why are truffles so rare?
And why don't we just farm more of them?
The answers lie in the truffle’s unique and somewhat mysterious biology.
Truffles refer to nearly 200 species of fungi,
of which around 30 are used in commercial trade.
The most sought-after truffles are native to Europe,
confined to regions with lime-rich dry soil and light summer rains.
Each species grows under the canopy of specific trees,
where they form tight symbiotic relationships.
A truffle’s mycelium, the vegetative root-like structure of the fungus,
wraps itself around the tree roots and swaps water and nutrients for sugars.
While truffles themselves aren’t mushrooms,
the parts of truffles we covet are, like mushrooms,
the fruiting body of the fungus.
But while mushrooms grow above ground,
allowing their spores to be dispersed by both wind and foraging animals,
truffles grow entirely below the soil.
So, they release a powerful aroma to lure hungry forest animals
to come to do their spreading.
If these animals happen to drop these spores near the roots
of a particular type of tree,
only then might a truffle develop, several years later.
This pungent aroma is also what’s made truffles alluring to humans.
In fact, much of what we perceive as truffle flavor
comes from its unique scent.
A single species typically emits 30 to 60 different volatile compounds,
which are produced by both the truffle and the bacteria and yeast
that colonize them.
But despite their scent,
finding these subterranean gems within a dense forest is far from easy.
As early as the 1400s, Europeans used pigs as truffle-finding companions.
Truffles emit dimethyl sulfide, a compound that reeks of cabbage,
which helps draw the pigs in.
But when a pig finds a truffle,
they’re apt to take a bite out of the profit.
So many truffle hunters began replacing pigs with dogs who,
while not natural-born truffle hunters, are more easily trained.
Meanwhile, others learn to track their prize by following species of flies
that are known to lay their eggs near truffles.
Nevertheless, today, the global market still demands more truffles
than any pig, dog, or fly can sniff out.
And truffles continue to grow even rarer— and more expensive—
as deforestation and climate change shrink their suitable terrain.
So why don't we just farm them?
We do, to some extent.
In 1808, a French farmer became the first to cultivate truffles
when he planted acorns under truffle-bearing oaks,
and then transplanted the seedlings with— unknowingly at the time—
some fungi companions.
By the early 1970s,
scientists cracked how to artificially inoculate tree seedlings,
which allowed growers to sow certain species of truffles
in orchards far outside their native range.
However, truffles are still not considered a domesticated crop.
There are just too many unknowns,
as researchers are still working out the precise temperature, precipitation,
elevation, soil pH, and other environmental factors
that determine a truffle orchard’s success.
Part of the problem is that truffles need to mate
to form that valuable fruiting body,
yet truffles sex is still largely a mystery.
Researchers know that these fungi are hermaphrodites
that cannot reproduce alone.
So, when a truffle reproduces,
one fungus takes on the maternal role and the other the paternal role,
each contributing half of the genes that will form the spores.
However, when researchers search the area near a truffle fruiting body,
they only ever find maternal mycelium.
Where the paternal partner lives— or hides— remains unknown.
So each time a hopeful truffle enthusiast begins a new farm,
there’s no guarantee truffles will grow,
and they'll have to wait four years or more to find out.
But many take the gamble,
as growers know that if they’re patient and lucky,
they might just dig up gold.

cuesZh.text: 在2007年秋天
一对托斯卡纳的父子发掘了 有史以来最大的一块白松露。
后来，它在拍卖会上 以创纪录的价格售出33万美元——
是当时其等重量黄金价格的六倍多。
松露是世界上最昂贵的食品之一 ——
部分原因是全球需求往往超过其供应。
但为什么松露是如此稀有？
那为什么我们不多种一些呢？
答案在于松露独特且有些神秘的生物学特性。
松露指的是近200种物种真菌，
其中约30种用于商业贸易。
最受追捧的松露原产于欧洲，
产地仅限于富含石灰的干燥土壤 和有轻微夏季降雨的地区。
每个物种都生长在特定树木的树冠下，
在那里它们形成紧密的共生关系。
松露的菌丝体，是真菌的营养根状结构，
它将自己包裹在树根上， 并将水和营养物质换成糖。
虽然松露本身不是蘑菇，
但我们垂涎的松露部分， 像蘑菇一样，
是真菌的子实体。
但是，当蘑菇生长在地上时，
使它们的孢子可以通过风和觅食动物散播，
而松露则完全生长在土壤之下。
因此，它们释放出一种强大的芳香剂， 引诱饥饿的森林动物
前来传播（孢子）。
如果这些动物碰巧将这些孢子落在
特定类型树的根部附近，
只有在那之后，松露才可能生长出来， 通常是在几年后。
这种强烈的香气也是松露吸引人类的原因。
实际上，我们所认为的松露味道在很大程度上
来自其独特的香味。
一个物种通常会释放 30 到 60种 不同的挥发性化合物，
这些化合物是由松露以及定殖在 其上的细菌和酵母菌
共同产生的。
但是，尽管它们的气味
在茂密的森林中 找到这些地下宝石绝非易事。
早在 1400 年代，欧洲人就使用猪 作为寻找松露的伙伴。
松露会释放二甲基硫化物， 这种化合物会散发像卷心菜的味道，
有助于吸引猪。
但是，当猪找到松露时，
它们很容易从利润中分一杯羹。
许多松露猎人开始用狗代替猪，
这些狗虽然不是天生的松露猎手， 但它们更容易训练。
与此同时，其他人通过跟踪一些已知会 在松露附近产卵的苍蝇种类，
来寻找他们的奖励。
然而，今天，全球市场对松露的需求仍然超过了
任何猪、狗或苍蝇能够嗅出的数量。
松露持续变得愈加稀有——也愈加昂贵——
随着森林砍伐和气候变化缩小了 它们适宜生长的区域
那么，为什么我们不直接种植它们呢？
在某种程度上，我们确实如此。
1808 年，一位法国农民 成为第一个种植松露的人，
他在含有松露的橡树下种下橡子，
然后将幼苗移植到——当时并不知道——
一些真菌伴侣身上。
到 1970 年代初，
科学家们破解了如何人工嫁接树苗，
这使得种植者能够在远离原产地的果园里
种植某些种类的松露。
但是，松露仍不被视为一种被驯化的作物。
因为还有太多未知的因素，
因为研究人员仍在努力研究 精确的温度、降水量、
海拔、土壤酸碱值及其他环境因素
这些都会决定松露果园成功的因素。
问题的一部分在于 松露需要交配
才能形成宝贵的子实体，
但松露的繁殖在很大程度上仍然是个谜。
研究人员知道，这些真菌是
无法单独繁殖的雌雄同体。
因此，当松露繁殖时，
一种真菌承担了母性的角色， 而另一种则承担了父性的角色，
每种真菌都贡献了形成孢子的基因的一半。
但是，当研究人员搜索松露子实体 附近的区域时，
他们只能找到母体菌丝体。
雄性伙伴的栖息地—— 或者说藏身之处——仍然未知。
因此，每当一个充满希望的 松露爱好者开始经营一个新的农场时，
松露是否能够生长并没有保证，
而且他们必须等四年或更长时间才能得到结果。
但是许多人选择了这场赌博，
因为种植者知道，如果他们有耐心和幸运的话，
他们可能会挖出黄金。
